summary.note: Both versions pass all assertions. The qualitative difference is in output quality: with-skill produces richer reasoning, correctly applies the 'config changes go with the feature' rule, and adds appropriate commit bodies. The worked example in the new skill appears to improve the model's reasoning transparency.

with_skill.qualitative_notes: Correct grouping, clear reasoning section, proper confirmation ask

old_skill.qualitative_notes: Correct grouping, slightly less detailed reasoning

with_skill.qualitative_notes: Added a body explaining idempotency design — good application of 'add body only for non-obvious context'

old_skill.qualitative_notes: Correct subject line, no body (also valid — body is optional)

with_skill.qualitative_notes: Correctly bundled package.json with profile feature per 'config changes go with feature' rule. Detailed per-commit reasoning. 4 commits total.
with_skill.notable_decision: package.json + package-lock.json bundled with feat(profile) — correct per skill guidance

old_skill.qualitative_notes: Split package.json into separate build(frontend): commit — debatable, slightly less aligned with skill guidance. 5 commits total.
old_skill.notable_decision: package.json + package-lock.json split into separate build: commit — less aligned with 'config changes go with feature' principle

analyst_notes: All assertions pass for both versions — the assertions may be too easy (non-discriminating). The real differences are qualitative.

The worked example in the new skill appears to improve reasoning quality: the with-skill run in eval 3 correctly applied the 'config changes go with the feature' principle, while the old skill split them out.
Eval 2 (staged changes) shows no meaningful difference — both versions handle the simple single-commit case identically.
Consider adding harder assertions that test the 'config changes go with feature' rule explicitly to better discriminate between versions.
The new skill's reasoning section in eval 3 output is notably more detailed — the worked example seems to encourage showing reasoning, not just results.
